---
title: "Predicting_concret_strength"
author: "Blake, Branson, Daylan, Emmett"
date: "2026-04-27"
output: pdf
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
install.packages(c("rstanarm", "bayesplot", "loo", "coda"))
```


## Load the libraries 

```{r}
options(timeout = 600)
install.packages("ggcorrplot")

install.packages("rstanarm")
install.packages("shiny")
install.packages("igraph")
library(rstanarm)
library(bayesplot)
library(loo)
library(coda)
library(ggcorrplot)

# Use multiple CPU cores so chains run in parallel (much faster)
options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())

# Set a seed so the results are reproducible
set.seed(42)



```

## Inspecting the dataset

```{r}
df <- load("concrete_data.RData")

print(df)

## Inspecting the dimensions
dim(concrete_train)

## Check the columns name :
names(concrete_train)
##
summary(concrete_train)



```

```{r}

## Checking for missing datas

# Any missing values in training data?
sum(is.na(concrete_train))

# Any missing values in test data (other than ccs which we expect)?
sum(is.na(concrete_test$ccs))           # should be 230
sum(is.na(concrete_test[, 1:8]))        # should be 0

```



```{r}
# Set up a 3x3 grid so we can see all 9 variables at once
par(mfrow = c(3, 3))

for (col in names(concrete_train)) {
  hist(concrete_train[[col]],
       main = col,
       xlab = col,
       col  = "steelblue",
       breaks = 30)
}

# Reset back to single plot
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


```
## Transforming age to log(age) to produce a more evenly distributed fit 

```{r}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(concrete_train$age, breaks = 30, col = "steelblue",
     main = "age (raw)", xlab = "age")
hist(log(concrete_train$age), breaks = 30, col = "darkorange",
     main = "log(age)", xlab = "log(age)")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```
## Check the correlation between log(age) vs ccs and age vs ccs :

```{r}
cor(concrete_train$age,      concrete_train$ccs)
cor(log(concrete_train$age), concrete_train$ccs)


par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(concrete_train$age, concrete_train$ccs,
     main = "ccs vs age", xlab = "age", ylab = "ccs",
     pch = 16, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.3))
plot(log(concrete_train$age), concrete_train$ccs,
     main = "ccs vs log(age)", xlab = "log(age)", ylab = "ccs",
     pch = 16, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.3))
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))



```
## Using log(age) as a predictor and drop raw age 

```{r}
# Create a working copy with log_age
train_work <- concrete_train
train_work$log_age <- log(train_work$age)
train_work$age <- NULL   # drop raw age

# Full correlation matrix, rounded for readability
round(cor(train_work), 2)


ggcorrplot(cor(train_work),
           type    = "upper",
           lab     = TRUE,
           lab_size = 3.5,
           method   = "square",
           colors   = c("#B2182B", "white", "#2166AC"),
           title    = "Correlation Matrix",
           ggtheme  = ggplot2::theme_minimal()) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1))
```

## Standardized the training and test variables :

```{r}
# Helper: add log_age column, drop raw age
prep_data <- function(df) {
  df$log_age <- log(df$age)
  df$age <- NULL
  df
}

train <- prep_data(concrete_train)
test  <- prep_data(concrete_test)

# Predictors (exactly what we'll feed the model)
predictors <- c("cement", "slag", "ash", "water", "superplasticizer",
                "coarse_agg", "fine_agg", "log_age")

# Compute means and SDs from TRAINING data only
mu <- sapply(train[, predictors], mean)
sg <- sapply(train[, predictors], sd)

# Apply standardization to both sets (same mu/sg for both!)
train_s <- train
test_s  <- test
for (v in predictors) {
  train_s[[v]] <- (train[[v]] - mu[v]) / sg[v]
  test_s[[v]]  <- (test[[v]]  - mu[v]) / sg[v]
}

# Sanity check: standardized predictors should have mean ≈ 0, sd ≈ 1 in TRAINING
round(colMeans(train_s[, predictors]), 3)   # all ~ 0
round(apply(train_s[, predictors], 2, sd), 3)  # all = 1


```

## Fit the model :

```{r}

fit_default <- stan_glm(
  ccs ~ cement + slag + ash + water + superplasticizer +
        coarse_agg + fine_agg + log_age,
  data    = train_s,
  family  = gaussian(),
  prior_intercept = normal(0, 2.5, autoscale = TRUE),
  prior           = normal(0, 2.5, autoscale = TRUE),
  prior_aux       = exponential(1, autoscale = TRUE),
  chains = 4,
  iter   = 4000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed   = 42
)
```

```{r}
# Full summary with credible intervals
print(fit_default, digits = 3)

# More detailed summary
summary(fit_default, digits = 3,
        probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))
```

## Visualizing 

```{r}

library(ggplot2)
# Posterior intervals for all coefficients (Section 3 plot)
mcmc_areas(as.matrix(fit_default),
           pars = c("cement", "slag", "ash", "water",
                    "superplasticizer", "coarse_agg",
                    "fine_agg", "log_age"),
           prob = 0.95) +
  ggtitle("Posterior 95% intervals for standardized coefficients")

# Trace plots — convergence visualization (Section 2 plot)
mcmc_trace(fit_default,
           pars = c("cement", "log_age", "water", "sigma"))
```
## Geweke statistic 


```{r}
# --- Geweke statistic ---
# |z| < 1.96 means no evidence of non-convergence (95% confidence)
draws_array <- as.array(fit_default)
mcmc_list <- coda::as.mcmc.list(
  lapply(seq_len(dim(draws_array)[2]),
         function(c) coda::as.mcmc(draws_array[, c, ]))
)
geweke_result <- coda::geweke.diag(mcmc_list)
print(geweke_result)


# --- Posterior predictive check ---
# Simulate datasets from the model and overlay on observed ccs
yrep <- posterior_predict(fit_default, draws = 200)

ppc_dens_overlay(y = train_s$ccs, yrep = yrep[1:50, ]) +
  ggtitle("PPC: simulated ccs (light) vs observed ccs (dark)")

ppc_scatter_avg(y = train_s$ccs, yrep = yrep) +
  ggtitle("PPC: average predicted vs observed ccs")
```
## Prior sensitivity 

```{r}
# Vague prior (almost flat — sd = 100, no autoscaling)
fit_vague <- update(fit_default,
                    prior          = normal(0, 100, autoscale = FALSE),
                    prior_intercept = normal(0, 100, autoscale = FALSE))

# Tight prior (strong shrinkage toward 0 — sd = 1, no autoscaling)
fit_tight <- update(fit_default,
                    prior = normal(0, 1, autoscale = FALSE))
```
```{r}
# Compare posterior means and SDs across priors
get_summary <- function(fit, label) {
  s <- summary(fit, digits = 3)
  data.frame(
    prior     = label,
    parameter = rownames(s),
    mean      = round(s[, "mean"], 3),
    sd        = round(s[, "sd"], 3)
  )
}

priors_compare <- rbind(
  get_summary(fit_default, "Default"),
  get_summary(fit_vague,   "Vague (sd=100)"),
  get_summary(fit_tight,   "Tight (sd=1)")
)

# Show only the regression coefficients (cleaner)
keep_params <- c("cement", "slag", "ash", "water",
                 "superplasticizer", "coarse_agg",
                 "fine_agg", "log_age", "sigma")
priors_compare <- priors_compare[priors_compare$parameter %in% keep_params, ]
priors_compare
```

# test the model against the test set 


```{r}
# 1. Generate posterior predictive draws for the 230 test observations
test_s <- test_s[, predictors]
pp_test <- posterior_predict(fit_default, newdata = test_s)
dim(pp_test)   # should be 12000 x 230 (12,000 posterior draws × 230 test points)

# 2. Compute posterior means (length-230 vector)
post_mean <- colMeans(pp_test)

# 3. Compute 95% credible intervals (230 x 2 matrix)
post_int <- t(apply(pp_test, 2, quantile, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)))
colnames(post_int) <- c("lower", "upper")

# --- SANITY CHECKS — these are critical before saving! ---
length(post_mean)             # MUST be 230
dim(post_int)                 # MUST be 230 by 2
range(post_mean)              # should be roughly within ccs training range (2-83)
mean(post_int[,2] - post_int[,1])   # average interval width (around 28-30 MPa)
head(post_mean)               # peek at first few predictions
head(post_int)                # peek at first few intervals

# 4. Save the submission file
save(post_mean, post_int, file = "40233_Group_3.RData")

# Verify the file exists
file.exists("40233_Group_3.RData")
```
```{r}
# Posterior predictive draws on the TRAINING set
yrep_train <- posterior_predict(fit_default, draws = 1000)

# Posterior mean prediction for each training observation
train_pred_mean <- colMeans(yrep_train)

# 95% credible intervals on training predictions
train_pred_int <- t(apply(yrep_train, 2, quantile, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)))

# --- KEY METRICS ---

# 1. Root Mean Squared Error
rmse_train <- sqrt(mean((train_s$ccs - train_pred_mean)^2))

# 2. Mean Absolute Error
mae_train <- mean(abs(train_s$ccs - train_pred_mean))

# 3. Bayesian R-squared
r2_train <- median(bayes_R2(fit_default))

# 4. Coverage of 95% intervals (should be ~0.95 if calibrated)
covered <- (train_s$ccs >= train_pred_int[, 1]) & (train_s$ccs <= train_pred_int[, 2])
coverage_train <- mean(covered)

# 5. Mean interval width
mean_width <- mean(train_pred_int[, 2] - train_pred_int[, 1])

# 6. Correlation between predictions and observations
pred_obs_cor <- cor(train_s$ccs, train_pred_mean)


cat("Training set performance:\n")
cat("--------------------------\n")
cat(sprintf("RMSE:                    %.3f MPa\n", rmse_train))
cat(sprintf("MAE:                     %.3f MPa\n", mae_train))
cat(sprintf("Bayesian R²:             %.3f\n",     r2_train))
cat(sprintf("Pred-obs correlation:    %.3f\n",     pred_obs_cor))
cat(sprintf("95%% interval coverage:   %.3f\n",     coverage_train))
cat(sprintf("Mean interval width:     %.3f MPa\n", mean_width))
cat(sprintf("Response range:          [%.2f, %.2f]\n",
            min(train_s$ccs), max(train_s$ccs)))
```
